title: Oriented Pearl-Necklace Arrays of Metallic Nanoparticles in Polymers: A New Route Toward Polarization-Dependent Color Filters
abstract:
action by mixing two micellar solutions containing the reactants. The copre-
cipitation takes place by mixing two micellar solutions having the same
water content, w = [H2O]/[AOT]: 0.1 M Na(AOT) containing Na2Te and a
mixed micellar solution containing Cd(AOT)2 [46] and Na(AOT). An ex-
cess of cadmium ions is used in the synthesis (x = [Cd2+]/[Te2±] = 2). The
overall concentrations of Na2Te and Cd(AOT)2 in micellar solution were 1.5
´ 10±4 and 3 ´ 10±4 M, respectively. The relative volume of each solution var-
ied. CdTe nanocrystals were formed a few seconds after these two degassed
micellar solutions were mixed. The syntheses were performed with droplets
of various sizes.
Procedure I: On addition of dodecanethiol to the micellar solution, a se-
lective surface reaction between the thio derivative and the cations took
place. The coated particles were then extracted from the micellar solution
and the surfactant used to form the colloidal dispersion was removed by ad-
dition of degassed ethanol. The coated particles precipitated. After centrifu-
gation, the substrate was removed. The coated particles are in powder form
and they are stable in air. The coated particles were then redispersed in a
mixture of two solvents (isopentane/methylcyclohexane, 3V/V) forming an
optically clear glass at low temperature. The coated particles dispersed in so-
lution were stable for a short period (7 days). When they were kept for long-
er, the stability of the particles changed markedly. In contrast, in powder
form, the particles are stable for several months.
Procedure II. This was the same as procedure I except that after the two
micellar solutions had been mixed, the particles were kept in micellar solu-
tion under nitrogen for 48 h. Then dodecanethiol was then added and the
particles extracted as before.
Remark: When syntheses were performed in the presence of excess tellur-
ide (x = [Cd2+]/[Te2±] = 1/2), CdTe nanoparticles were formed. However,
after dodecanethiol addition and during the washing process, a black precip-
itate appeared, due to the formation of tellurium oxide. It was then impossi-
ble to separate the CdTe nanoparticles from TeO2 aggregates.

Final version: August 6, 1998

Oriented Pearl-Necklace Arrays of Metallic
Nanoparticles in Polymers: A New Route
Toward Polarization-Dependent Color Filters**
By Yvo Dirix,* Cees Bastiaansen, Walter Caseri, and
Paul Smith
Polarizers and color filters are routinely used in conven-
tional liquid-crystal displays (LCDs) to generate visual per-
ception of the switching of the electrooptical cell and to
generate colors.[1±3] Both the polarizers and color filters ab-
sorb a substantial portion of the incident light and, conse-
quently, the light efficiency, brightness, and operating life
of battery-powered LCDs are rather poor.[2±4] Recently,
several improvements to this unsatisfactory situation have
been developed or proposed. For example, advances have
been made in linear polarizers, which now directly transmit
one polarization direction of light and reflect the other.[5,6]
The reflected light is subsequently depolarized and re-
cycled using appropriate mirrors and diffusers, which
strongly enhances the light efficiency of the polarizers.
Also, linear polarizers were produced that not only emit
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 3
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/0302-0223 $ 17.50+.50/0
223

[*] Dr. Y. Dirix, Dr. C. Bastiaansen, Prof. W. Caseri, Prof. P. Smith
Department of Materials, ETH Zürich
CH-8092 Zürich (Switzerland)
[**] The authors thank the Swiss National Science Foundation and the
ETH Zürich (Templated Materials Project) for their financial support.
 Communications
224
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/0302-0224 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 3
polarized light with a high efficiency but also generate
bright colors in the visible wavelength range.[7±9] These
polarizers are based on oriented polymeric films containing
dichroic, fluorescent guest molecules. To maximize the light
efficiency, a second, non-dichroic fluorescent guest mol-
ecule is added, which absorbs all the incident unpolarized
light and transfers the optical energy to the dichroic fluor-
escent guest molecule.[9] Again, the theoretical light effi-
ciency of the polarizers is enhanced in a suitable electroop-
tical device, and bright colors are produced simultaneously
without using additional light- and energy-consuming color
filters. The brightness and energy efficiency of LCDs can
potentially be further enhanced if a dark, light-absorbing
(on or off) state can be avoided and if two or three (pri-
mary) colors can be generated in a single optical compo-
nent (see below). More specifically, those color filters are
of interest that have an absorption spectrum that depends
on the polarization direction of incident light, which allows
for the generation of multiple colors in a single electroopti-
cal element. The appropriate optical characteristics are, to
some extent, already described in the literature, but a sim-
ple technological route to produce large-area, flexible poly-
mer films based on synthetic polymers with the desired
optical characteristics appears not to be available.[10±19]
Here, a new general route is presented to produce such
films, which is illustrated with the preparation of oriented
polyethylene containing silver nanoparticles.
Silver nanoparticles were produced according to (slightly
modified) literature procedures by the reduction of silver
trifluoromethanesulfonate in toluene.[20,21] The silver salt
was reduced by sodium borohydride, which was transferred
from the aqueous to the organic phase by the phase trans-
fer agent tetraoctylammonium bromide (see Experimental
section). Silver particles with a surface-stabilizing layer
(dodecanethiol) are obtained, which introduces ªdispersa-
bilityº of the colloidal metal particles in common organic
solvents such as toluene or xylene. Moreover, in the meth-
od used, byproducts related to the reduction of the silver
salt are completely removed from the stabilized silver par-
ticles. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images re-
veal that virtually monodisperse particles are formed, the
average size of the silver core being approximately 4.5 nm
(Fig. 1a). The stability of the dodecanethiol layer on the sil-
ver particles was investigated using thermogravimetric
analysis (TGA). No weight loss was observed below ap-
proximately 180 C in a nitrogen atmosphere, which illus-
trates that the nanoparticles' high-temperature stability is
excellent. The silver content of the particles was deter-
mined from the residual weight after heating to 600 C. A
silver weight fraction of 71 wt.-% was derived, which corre-
sponds to a silver volume fraction of approximately
17 vol.-%. Solution-cast films were prepared by dispersing
various amounts of the silver nanoparticles (10±100 mg) in
50 mL of p-xylene. High-density polyethylene (HDPE,
Hostalen Gur 7225 P, MW = 400 kg/mol, 0.5 g) was added,
and the mixture was degassed at reduced pressure and
heated to 130 C for 15 min while stirring until a homoge-
neous dark brown liquid was obtained. The latter was cast
and dried overnight under ambient conditions. After
evaporation of the p-xylene, the films were compression-
molded for 15 min at 180 C. TEM images of the undrawn
nanocomposites illustrate that the nanoparticles are rather
poorly dispersed in the polymer (i.e., the silver particles
tend to agglomerate in the polymer matrix; see Fig. 1b).
The melt-crystallized films were drawn on a hot shoe at
120 C; the draw ratio was determined from the displace-
ment of ink marks. In the drawn sample (Fig. 1c), arrays of
spherical particles are observed that are aligned in the
Fig. 1. TEM micrographs of a) alkanethiol-coated silver nanoparticles,
b) undrawn polyethylene/silver nanocomposites containing 4 wt.-% nano-
particles, and c) drawn nanocomposite (draw ratio = 15); the draw direction
is horizontal.

mined from the residual weight after heating to 600 C. A

sponds to a silver volume fraction of approximately

neous dark brown liquid was obtained. The latter was cast

molded for 15 min at 180 C. TEM images of the undrawn

120 C; the draw ratio was determined from the displace-
ment of ink marks. In the drawn sample (Fig. 1c), arrays of

Fig. 1. TEM micrographs of a) alkanethiol-coated silver nanoparticles,
b) undrawn polyethylene/silver nanocomposites containing 4 wt.-% nano-
particles, and c) drawn nanocomposite (draw ratio = 15); the draw direction
drawing direction and have a rather high aspect ratio, i.e.,
the ratio of the length to the width of the arrays (>25). Evi-
dently, the aggregates of silver particles in the films prior to
drawing deform into pearl-necklace-type arrays during the
solid-state drawing of the nanocomposites.
The UV-vis absorption spectra of the undrawn and
drawn nanocomposites in unpolarized light are shown in
Figure 2. Prior to drawing, the films exhibit a single absorp-
tion maximum at a wavelength of 435 nm (Fig. 2a), and
after solid-state drawing two absorption maxima are
observed at wavelengths of 420 and 483 nm (Fig. 2b). The
absolute value of the absorption maximum (420 nm) is
lowered due to a reduction of the film thickness as a result
of the drawing process. The UV-vis spectra of a drawn
nanocomposite in linearly polarized light are shown in
Figure 2c. Clearly, the drawn nanocomposite exhibits a
strong dependence of the absorption spectrum on the angle
j between the polarization direction of the incident light
and the drawing direction of the films. Light vibrating par-
allel to the drawing axis (j = 0) is absorbed at higher
wavelengths, with an absorption maximum at 489 nm,
whereas light vibrating perpendicular to this axis (j = 90)
is absorbed at lower wavelengths, with an absorption maxi-
mum at 398 nm. An isosbestic point is observed in the ab-
sorption spectra at a wavelength of 425 nm. In Figure 3,
photographs are shown of drawn silver/HDPE nanocom-
posites that were taken in linearly polarized light. In accor-
dance with the UV-vis measurements, it is found that the
color of the films depends strongly on the polarization di-
rection of the incident light. In fact, the films exhibit bright
colors and appear yellow or red, depending on the polariza-
tion direction of the incident light.
Previously, several other materials containing elemental
nanoparticles were reported to exhibit a strongly polariza-
tion-dependent color. For instance, the dichroism of plant
fibrils containing reduced metal salts was investigated ex-
tensively by Frey-Wyssling.[13,14] Polarization-dependent
colors were observed, and it was assumed that this origi-
nates from a chain-like ordering of spherical metal nano-
particles in the plant fibrils. In the 1940s and 1950s, Land,
alone and together with West,[15,16] investigated the optical
properties of metal nanocomposites produced by the in situ
reduction of metal salts in oriented poly(vinyl alcohol). It
was argued that the observed polarization-dependent ab-
sorption spectra originate from the formation of oriented
metallic needles in the polymer matrix. More recently, Lu
et al.[19] investigated the dichroism of arrays of gold nano-
particles that were produced by controlled deposition of
elemental metal particles onto oriented polymeric sub-
strates. They observed chain-like ordering in the thiol-coat-
ed gold particles, resulting in only very weak dichroism,
presumably due to electrical isolation of the separate parti-
cles. Strong dichroism was observed after removal of the
polymer substrate by burning in a torch at 1700 C, and it
was again suggested that the observed optical phenomena
originate from the formation of metallic needles during the
heat treatment. In the present study arrays of thiol-coated,
electrically isolated silver particles are produced in a poly-
mer film, and a strong dichroism is nonetheless observed
(Fig. 2c), suggesting that bulk electrical conductivity is not

a prerequisite for generating the desired optical phenome-
In Figure 4a, a schematic diagram is presented of a
twisted-nematic electrooptical cell that switches between a
transparent (or colored) off-state and a dark, light-absorb-
ing on-state. A schematic diagram of a similar electro-
optical cell that is fitted with a polarization-dependent
color filter is shown in Figure 4b. In comparison to a classi-
cal LCD, the top polarizer (analyzer) and color filter are
replaced by a single optical film with a polarization-depen-
dent color. A photograph of a display that is constructed
according to the latter diagram is shown in Figure 4c. The
display transmits colored light in both the on- and off-
states, which enhances the brightness and light efficiency; a
dark on-state in which light is absorbed and not emitted is
In summary, a new route to produce polarization-depen-
dent color filters is described that is based on the formation
of HDPE containing elemental silver nanoparticles. Flexible
films are obtained containing pearl-necklace-type arrays of
metal nanoparticles that are oriented with their long axis in
the drawing direction and have a high aspect ratio (>25). It is
shown that the films exhibit a color that is strongly depen-
dent on the polarization direction of the incident light. These
films are potentially useful in manufacturing LCDs with an
enhanced brightness and energy efficiency.
The main merits of the new route to polarization-depen-
dent color filters are that flexible polymeric films are ob-
tained (unlike Ambronn and Zsigmondy,[10,11] Frey-Wys-
sling,[13,14] and Lu et al.[19]) without byproducts originating
from the reduction of the metal salts (Land and West[15,16]),
and that rather bright colors are generated. The color fil-
ters presented here are fabricated using conventional solu-
tion casting/drawing techniques that, in principle, can be
readily scaled up for the production of large-area films.
Moreover, it is possible to produce films that exhibit all the
Fig. 3. Photographs of drawn polyethylene/silver nanocomposites in polar-

primary colors by varying the size of the arrays of metal
nanoparticles (width, length), or by using different metals.
A more detailed study concerning color tuning of these
films by selecting proper materials and processing condi-
tions will be presented separately.
were synthesized according to slightly modified versions of methods
(CF3SO3Ag, 9 × 10±4 mol), tetraoctylammonium bromide ([C8H17]4NBr, 4 ×
10±3 mol), and 1-dodecanethiol (C12H25SH, 1.8 × 10±3 mol) were dissolved in
80 mL of toluene. A freshly prepared aqueous sodium borohydride solution
(1 × 10±2 mol in 25 mL of distilled water) was slowly added under vigorous
stirring. The organic layer was separated, and the solution was concentrated
by solvent evaporation to a volume of approximately 5 mL. Then, 500 mL
of ethanol was added at ±20 C. After sedimentation of the particles, the out-
standing solution was decanted, and the residue was again treated with
500 mL of ethanol at ±20 C. Finally, the particles were dried for 12 h at
room temperature and under reduced pressure.
TGA was performed with a Netzsch TG 209 instrument. The nanopar-
ticles (ca. 10 mg) were heated under a nitrogen flow at a rate of 10 C/min
TEM was performed using a Philips EM 301 electron microscope. A drop
of the toluene reaction mixture containing the colloidal metal particles was
placed onto a carbon film supported by a copper grid. After evaporation of
the solvent, the samples were inserted into the electron microscope. The un-
drawn and drawn polyethylene/silver nanocomposites (containing 4 wt.-%
nanoparticles) were cut at low (±80 C) temperatures using a diamond knife;
the thin slices were collected on a copper grid and used for TEM.
Polarized UV-vis spectra were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer Lambda
900 instrument, fitted with motor-driven Glan±Thomson polarizers. The
nanocomposites were sandwiched between two quartz glass slides and
coated with a small amount of silicone oil in order to minimize light scatter-
ing at the film surfaces [22].

Self-Assembly Processes for Organic LED
Electrode Passivation and Charge Injection
By Joshua E. Malinsky, Ghassan E. Jabbour,
Sean E. Shaheen, Jeffrey D. Anderson, Andrew G. Richter,
Tobin J. Marks,* Neal R. Armstrong, Bernard Kippelen,
Pulak Dutta, and Nasser Peyghambarian
Since the initial reports of thin-film organic light-emit-
ting diodes (OLEDs),[1] there has been intense interest in
understanding and enhancing the operating performance
characteristics of such devices. This interest reflects the
fundamental challenge of understanding charge transport
and luminescence in organic solids as well as the great po-
tential OLEDs offer for large-area, low-cost, full color, flat
panel display products.[2±5] The basic fabrication of OLED
structures consists of the vapor or solution phase deposi-
tion of organic molecules/macromolecules onto a transpar-
ent conductive oxide electrode such as indium tin oxide
(ITO). Basic two-layer devices consist of a hole transport
layer (HTL) deposited directly onto ITO followed by an
emissive/electron transport layer (ETL) deposited on the
HTL. A low work-function metal cathode completes the
OLED structure.[6±9] Although the exact mechanisms are
not fully understood, it has been observed recently that the
performance characteristics of these types of OLED struc-
tures can be substantially enhanced by modifying the or-
ganic thin film±electrode interfaces in various ways.[10±17]
In this communication, we report the use of layer-by-
layer, self-limiting chemisorptive siloxane self-assembly
techniques[18±21] to introduce conformal, microstructurally/
electronically well-defined passivating/charge injection bal-
ancing dielectric layers between the problematic ITO an-
ode[22±24] and the HTL of a two-layer OLED device. We
show that such self-assembly techniques can yield confor-
